coredata.dc:description: MXenes quantum dots (QDs), including Nb2C, Nb2CO2, and Nb2CF2, are emerging materials with exceptional structural, electronic, and optical properties, making them highly suitable for biomedical applications. This study investigates the structural optimization, stability, electronic properties, and drug-loading potential of these QDs using fluorouracil (Flu) as a model drug. Structural analyses show that the functionalization of Nb2C with O and F atoms enhances stability, with binding energies (BEs) of 7.335, 8.154, and 6.704 eV for Nb2C, Nb2CO2, and Nb2CF2, respectively. The drug-loading study reveals that Nb2C exhibits the highest adsorption energy of −6.775 eV at the surface site (2.053 Å), while Nb2CO2 and Nb2CF2 demonstrate weaker interactions with adsorption energies of −2.163 eV and −0.933 eV, respectively. Non-covalent interaction (NCI) and natural bond orbital (NBO) analyses show significant changes in electron density distribution upon drug interaction, with the natural charge on the O7 atom in Flu shifting slightly upon interaction. Optical property investigations indicate a blue shift in the absorption spectra for Nb2CO2 (λ max = 764.76 nm) and Nb2CF2 (λ max = 1108.71 nm), compared to Nb2C (λ max = 2612.00 nm), confirming the tunability of these materials for therapeutic applications. By addressing key challenges in drug delivery, such as stability, controlled release, and interaction strength, this study establishes Nb2CO2 and Nb2CF2 as promising nanocarriers, with the potential to improve drug efficacy and minimize side effects in targeted cancer therapies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–d) Front and top view of the relaxed structures of Nb2C, Nb2CO2, Nb2CF2, and fluorouracil. (e) Symbol colors used. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(a–c) Calculated IR spectra of Nb2C and its based materials.
HOMO and LUMO, along with their corresponding energies and the energy gap (Eg), for Nb2C, Nb2CO2, and Nb2CF2.
(a–f) The optimized structures of Flu drug loading on Nb2C and its based materials at both edge and surface.
(a–f) HOMO/LUMO representation, HOMO energy, LUMO energy, and energy gap of Nb2C and its based materials after the drug loading.
The calculated absorption of Nb2C and its derived materials, both before and after the adsorption of Flu at the edge and surface sites.

(a–c) NCI plot and NCI surface of Nb2C and its based materials after the surface loading of Flu drug. The reduced density gradient (RDG) is a measure used in electronic structure analysis. Here, ρ represents the electron density, λ 2 denotes the second eigenvalue of the Hessian matrix, and "sign" indicates whether is positive or negative.
Structural parameters including bond length (Å), dihedral, and bond angles (degrees) of Nb2C, Nb2CO2, and Nb2CF2.
The adsorption energy (Eads) of the Flu drug and the Nb2C and its derivatives and the closest distance (dQD-Flu) between the Flu and QD.

Important optical parameters including, maximum wavelength (λ max), excited state (ES), transition energy (TE), electronic transition (ET), oscillator strength (f), and transition coefficient (TC) for Nb2C, its based materials prior to and following the adsorption of Flu at both edge and surface.
The charge, population, and electronic configuration of the O7 atom in the Flu drug were investigated both prior to and following its adsorption onto Nb2C and its derivatives.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: MXenes quantum dots (QDs), including Nb2C, Nb2CO2, and Nb2CF2, are emerging materials with exceptional structural, electronic, and optical properties, making them highly suitable for biomedical applications. This study investigates the structural optimization, stability, electronic properties, and drug-loading potential of these QDs using fluorouracil (Flu) as a model drug. Structural analyses show that the functionalization of Nb2C with O and F atoms enhances stability, with binding energies (BEs) of 7.335, 8.154, and 6.704 eV for Nb2C, Nb2CO2, and Nb2CF2, respectively. The drug-loading study reveals that Nb2C exhibits the highest adsorption energy of −6.775 eV at the surface site (2.053 Å), while Nb2CO2 and Nb2CF2 demonstrate weaker interactions with adsorption energies of −2.163 eV and −0.933 eV, respectively. Non-covalent interaction (NCI) and natural bond orbital (NBO) analyses show significant changes in electron density distribution upon drug interaction, with the natural charge on the O7 atom in Flu shifting slightly upon interaction. Optical property investigations indicate a blue shift in the absorption spectra for Nb2CO2 (λ max = 764.76 nm) and Nb2CF2 (λ max = 1108.71 nm), compared to Nb2C (λ max = 2612.00 nm), confirming the tunability of these materials for therapeutic applications. By addressing key challenges in drug delivery, such as stability, controlled release, and interaction strength, this study establishes Nb2CO2 and Nb2CF2 as promising nanocarriers, with the potential to improve drug efficacy and minimize side effects in targeted cancer therapies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Because of their potential for targeted drug therapy, biosensing, and disease detection, the creation of innovative two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterials has attracted a lot of interest in therapeutics and biomedicine [1–3]. Drug delivery is one of these uses that seems to have the most promise. High drug loading capacity is made possible by the vast surface area of 2D nanomaterials [4], and sustained drug release is made possible by their robust interactions with drug molecules [5]. They are useful in the development of innovative biomedical technologies because their varied chemical compositions also allow for intelligent drug release that is driven by external inputs [6]. For uses including gene transportation [7], photothermal therapy [8], tissue engineering [9], and anticancer drug delivery [10–12], graphene and its derivatives have been thoroughly investigated.

In the last decade, the potential of graphene [13], MoS2 [14], GeSe [15], biphenylene [16] and phosphorene [17] for drug delivery in tumor therapy has been increasingly highlighted. However, the intricate mechanisms of drug loading and release in 2D materials require further exploration. Complementary atomic-scale studies can provide deeper theoretical insights into the stability, charge transfer properties, solubility, and responsiveness of these materials when interacting with drugs. For instance, Song et al. [18] used Density Functional Theory (DFT) to investigate the interaction of Doxorubicin with graphene and graphene oxide, revealing strong adsorption and significant affinity, which suggests their wide-ranging potential applications. In addition to these promising 2D materials, other systems such as hexagonal boron nitride (BN) and graphite carbon nitride (g-C3N4) have also garnered attention due to their unique properties. BN, like graphene, exhibits high thermal conductivity, exceptional mechanical strength, thermal stability, large surface area, oxidation resistance, and chemical inertness [19]. These characteristics make BN materials suitable for a variety of applications, including catalysts [20], dielectric materials [21], sensors [22], composite materials [23], and hydrogen storage [24]. More recently, BN materials have been investigated for their potential in drug delivery systems [25–27]. For example, Gnatyuk et al. explored 2D-BN nanoparticles for delivering the anticancer drug doxorubicin (DOX) [28]. The development of effective drug delivery systems for anticancer agents, including 5-fluorouracil (FU), 6-mercaptopurine (MP), and 6-thioguanine (TG), is essential for overcoming the limitations of these drugs, such as poor bioavailability, low solubility, and high systemic toxicity. Improving their targeted delivery could significantly enhance their therapeutic efficacy and minimize adverse effects, thereby advancing chemotherapy strategies [29–33].

Nanotechnology has gained significant attention for its potential in drug delivery, utilizing a variety of nanostructures such as carbon-based and silicon materials, polymers, liposomes, dendrimers, magnetic nanoparticles, and niosomes. These materials offer unique properties that make them ideal candidates for advanced delivery systems [34,35]. For instance, Saberinasab et al. [36] demonstrated the utility of polyethylene glycol-functionalized carbon nanotubes (CNT-PEG) in delivering temozolomide (TMZ), where the interaction between the drug and the nanotubes enhanced the stability of the therapeutic. Similarly, Liang and Luo [37] explored the use of MoS2 nanosheets for a combination of photothermal and chemotherapy, highlighting the promise of MoS2-PEG complexes in drug delivery due to their strong interactions with the drug and their surface characteristics. While 2D materials like these show considerable potential in nanobiotechnology, their application in clinical settings, particularly for targeted drug delivery, demands careful attention to their biodegradability and toxicity [38,39]. Understanding the physicochemical interactions between these materials and the drugs they carry is essential for creating biocompatible delivery systems. Such systems could not only enhance the delivery of therapeutic agents but also enable improved biomedical imaging, sensing, and gene regulation.

With an emphasis on their potential as drug delivery systems, the development of nanomaterials for cancer therapy has attracted a lot of attention recently. To treat tumors, for example, functionalized nanomaterials, such as MXenes, have demonstrated potential for targeted delivery and controlled drug release [40]. The interactions between medicinal molecules and nanomaterials have been extensively studied using DFT, which offers vital information on binding affinities and electronic structures [41]. More recently, the passivation of nanomaterials has been shown to increase drug loading stability and efficiency, opening up new possibilities for the design of effective delivery systems [42,43]. It is crucial to comprehend these materials' electrical characteristics since they might have a big influence on how well they work in drug delivery systems [44,45].

MXene, a new class of layered 2D materials derived from metal carbides or nitrides, follows the general formula MN+1ANTx, where "M" represents an early transition metal, "A" refers to carbon, nitrogen, or both, and "T" is a surface group such as O, OH, or F [46–48]. These materials exhibit a combination of covalent, metallic, and ionic bonds, which contribute to their enhanced structural resilience [12]. MXenes are known for their exceptional properties, including metallic conductivity, large specific surface areas, high electron density, hydrophilicity, and surface functional groups. These characteristics make MXenes highly suitable for a wide range of applications, such as photo-electrocatalysis, therapeutics, water treatment, biosensing, diagnostics, and photothermal therapy [49–52]. Their environmental potential is also significant, due to their hydrophilicity, structural integrity, high melting points, oxidation resistance, chemical stability, and superior thermal and electrical conductivity [53–55]. Furthermore, the combination of MXenes with magnetic materials has led to the development of effective magnetic-MXene-based nanocomposites [56]. Nb2C functionalization offers distinct advantages over other MXenes in drug delivery systems [57,58]. Nb2C has remarkable mechanical and electrical characteristics as well as adjustable surface chemistry, which is further improved by functionalization with groups like -O and -F. These changes enhance their electrical properties, stability, and adsorption capacity, which makes them especially useful for applications involving controlled drug administration. Nb2C offers a more stable drug delivery platform than extensively researched MXenes like Ti3C2 [58,59], which might not have the same stability or adsorption efficacy.

The unique benefits of Nb2C functionalization in drug delivery systems compared to other MXenes. Nb2C exhibits exceptional electronic and mechanical properties, alongside tunable surface chemistry, which are enhanced further through functionalization with groups such as -O and -F. These modifications improve their stability, adsorption capacity, and electronic characteristics, making them particularly advantageous for controlled drug delivery applications. Compared to widely studied MXenes like Ti3C2, which may lack similar stability or adsorption effectiveness, Nb2C provides a more robust platform for drug delivery. With emphasis on both edge and surface sites, we examine the adsorption characteristics and electrical interactions of fluorouracil (Flu) with Nb2C-based MXenes in this work. The use of computational techniques to evaluate the effects of passivation on the stability, electronic structure, and adsorption performance of Nb2C MXenes—which have mostly been investigated for energy storage and catalysis in the past—makes this work unique. We hope to demonstrate MXenes' potential for drug delivery applications—a largely untapped area—by assessing the drug-material interaction in this context. Additionally, this study offers fresh perspectives on how surface functionalization enhances Nb2C's stability and reactivity, providing useful information for the creation of effective drug delivery systems.

In this study, we utilized Gaussian 16 software [60] to perform density functional theory (DFT) [61,62] simulations on Nb2C, Nb2CO2, and Nb2CF2 MXenes both before and after the adsorption of the drug fluorouracil (Flu). DFT was used for its ability to accurately predict electronic properties, adsorption energies, and molecular interactions. It efficiently models non-covalent interactions and charge transfer, crucial for understanding drug-material behavior [41,43]. DFT strikes a balance between computational efficiency and precision, making it ideal for drug delivery systems [44,45]. Our focus was on optimizing structures, examining electronic properties, and analyzing optical behaviors to explore potential applications in drug delivery. We employed the hybrid M06 functional [63,64], recognized for its accuracy in predicting optimized structures, electronic states, and optical properties [65–68]. The LANL2DZ basis set [69] was chosen for its balance of computational efficiency and accuracy [70,71]. The M06/LANL2DZ level of theory was applied to both the MXene materials and the drug molecules. This approach ensures consistent computational treatment of both the materials and the drugs during the simulations. The M06 functional and LANL2DZ basis set were chosen for their suitability in modeling non-covalent interactions and electronic properties in transition metal systems like Nb2C [72,73]. These methods ensure accurate representation of Flu adsorption while maintaining computational efficiency, aligning with established practices in similar studies. While CAM-B3LYP is preferred for excited-state energies and optical properties, M06 was chosen for its proven accuracy in similar contexts. For optical property improvements, future studies could explore CAM-B3LYP, as suggested in previous works [74–76]. To account for van der Waals interactions, which are crucial for accurately characterizing interactions between MXene materials and the drug, we incorporated Grimme's dispersion correction (gd3) into the M06 functional [77]. This enhancement improves the treatment of non-covalent interactions (NCI) [78], resulting in a more precise system description. Additionally, we conducted time-dependent DFT simulations to thoroughly analyze optical properties, focusing on the first fifty excited states [79]. The drug molecule was initially positioned 4 Å above the edge and surface of the MXene. The original random orientation of the drug in this setup allows it to move freely during the adsorption process (optimization steps) to achieve the most stable configuration. This approach ensures that the optimized structures represent the energetically favorable orientations of the drug when adsorbed on the surface or edge. Adsorption energies were calculated for both cases to evaluate the interaction strength.

Fig. 1 (a–d) shows the front and side views of the optimized structures of the MXenes quantum dots (QDs), namely Nb2C, Nb2CO2, and Nb2CF2. The front views of Nb2C, Nb2CO2, and Nb2CF2 after optimization show no deformation, indicating the high strength of these compounds. The side view of Nb2C reveals a single square ring in thickness. However, after adding O and F atoms to form Nb2CO2 and Nb2CF2, the edge thickness increases to two square rings at the edges and three square rings in the center of both structures. The side views also reveal a nonplanar, zigzag surface area. Structural parameters such as bond lengths (Å), dihedral angles, and bond angles (degrees) for the optimized structures are presented in Table 1 . Due to the high electronegativity of F and O atoms, the Nb1-O3 and Nb1-O5 bonds in Nb2CO2, and the F1-Nb3 and F1-Nb5 bonds in Nb2CF2, are longer than the Nb1-C2 and Nb1-C4 bonds in Nb2C. The dihedral angles also indicate the nonplanarity of the surfaces, with the following values: C6-Nb1-C2-Nb9 in Nb2C is 177.072°, O5-Nb1-O3-Nb9 in Nb2CO2 is 169.937°, and Nb5-F1-Nb3-Nb8 in Nb2CF2 is 175.445°. Fig. 1d and e shows the optimized structure of fluorouracil (Flu) and the color coding for the symbols used in all studied structures. Before loading the Flu drug onto the carrier structures, the following structural parameters are noted: the N1-C2 and N1-C6 bond lengths are 1.394 Å and 1.411 Å, respectively, the C6-N1-C1 bond angle is 128.396°, and the F9-C3-C2-N1 dihedral angle is 179.997°, indicating that the F and O atoms of the carbonyl groups lie in the same plane as the diazine ring (see Fig. 1d). All optimized geometries, including the detailed atomic coordinates for each configuration, have been included in the supplementary materials.

The stability of the compounds under investigation (Nb2C and its derivatives) is assessed using the binding energy (BE), obtained using the equation: BE = (ENbNNb + ECNC + ExNx – ET)/NT, where ENb, EC, Ex, and ET is the energy of Nb, C, functional atom (F or O), and the total energy respectively. NNb, NC, Nx, and NT are the number of Nb, C, functional atom (F or O), and total number of the atoms, respectively. The calculated BE values for Nb2C, Nb2CO2, and Nb2CF2 are 7.335, 8.154, and 6.704 eV, respectively. Based on these BE values, the stability of Nb2CO2 is higher than that of Nb2C and Nb2CF2. Therefore, adding O atoms to Nb2C enhances its stability, while adding F atoms to Nb2C increases its reactivity. Additionally, the positive BE values indicate the inherent stability of Nb2C and its derivatives. The enhanced stability of Nb2CO2, demonstrated by strong BE, ensures its reliability as a drug delivery platform. This stability minimizes premature drug release or degradation, making it suitable for controlled drug delivery systems. Its ability to maintain structure under physiological conditions enhances its potential for sustained drug interaction, positioning Nb2CO2 as an ideal candidate for therapeutic applications.

IR spectra play a crucial role in understanding molecular structure, identifying functional groups, and confirming the presence of specific chemical bonds. Therefore, we calculated the IR spectra to analyze the effect of functionalization on IR behavior and to confirm the stability of the studied compounds Nb2C, Nb2CO2, and Nb2CF2. The resulting IR spectra are depicted in Fig. 2 (a–c). For Nb2C, the IR spectrum appears within the range of 100–800 cm−1, as shown in Fig. 2a. This range is characteristic of various stretching and bending modes of the Nb-C bonds. Upon functionalizing Nb₂C with oxygen to form Nb₂CO₂, new IR peaks at ∼963 cm⁻1 and 1400 cm⁻1 (Fig. 2b) indicate Nb–O stretching, confirming oxygen incorporation. These frequencies exceed the typical range of 600–900 cm⁻1 [80], likely due to altered bonding environments. The 1400 cm⁻1 peak may also involve overtones or combined modes, reflecting the complexity of functionalized Nb-based MXenes. Additionally, when Nb2C is functionalized with fluorine atoms, a new IR peak appears around 895 cm−1, as depicted in Fig. 2c, corresponding to the Nb-F stretching mode. These observations highlight the significant changes in the vibrational spectra due to functionalization, providing insights into the altered bonding environments. Furthermore, the existence of positive vibrational frequencies for all the compounds studied confirms their thermodynamic stability, as shown in Fig. 2(a–c). This stability is important for potential applications, as it ensures that the functionalized materials maintain their structural integrity under various conditions.

The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) distributions of Nb2C and its derivatives (Nb2CO2 and Nb2CF2) are presented in Fig. 3 . HOMO-LUMO characteristics: (1) In Nb2C, the HOMO is localized on the bond between niobium (Nb) and carbon (C) atoms, indicating a strong overlap between the d orbitals of Nb and the p orbitals of C. LUMO is likely to make a significant contribution from the empty d orbitals of the Nb atom. (2) In Nb2CO2, oxygen, being more electronegative than both Nb and C, attracts electrons more strongly. Consequently, the HOMO in Nb2CO2 has a greater contribution from the orbitals of the O atom compared to Nb2C, resulting in the delocalization of the HOMO towards the O atom. Conversely, the LUMO may be more localized on the Nb or C atoms, as they are better electron acceptors than O. (3) In Nb2CF2, fluorine, the most electronegative element, pulls electrons even more strongly than O in Nb2CO2, further delocalizing the HOMO towards the F atoms. Like Nb2CO2, the LUMO might be more localized on the Nb or C atoms. As shown in Fig. 3, the incorporation of O and F atoms into Nb2C decreases both the HOMO and LUMO energies, thereby widening the HOMO-LUMO gap (Eg), which indicates lower chemical reactivity or greater difficulty in light excitation for the functionalized molecules. The discrepancy between the BE and Eg can be explained by the electronic properties of the MXenes. Nb₂C has the lowest Eg (0.938 eV), indicating high reactivity and lower stability. Nb₂CO₂, with an Eg of 1.496 eV, shows higher stability and reduced reactivity due to oxygen modification. Nb₂CF₂ (Eg = 1.314 eV) exhibits intermediate reactivity and stability. These results suggest that oxygen and fluorine modifications enhance stability but affect electronic behavior, with Nb₂CO₂ being the most stable, followed by Nb₂CF₂, and Nb₂C showing the highest reactivity. Passivation with O and F significantly alters Nb2C's electronic properties. For example, Nb2CO2 shows a higher Eg (1.496 eV) than pristine Nb2C (0.938 eV), indicating enhanced electronic stability, while Nb2CF2 (1.314 eV) reflects the influence of fluorine. BE also increases with passivation, as seen with Nb2CO2 (8.154 eV) versus Nb2C (7.335 eV), indicating improved stability. These changes enhance Nb2C's chemical reactivity and stability, tailoring it for drug delivery.

Fig. 4 (a–f) presents the optimized structures of Flu drug adsorbed onto Nb2C and its derivatives, illustrating both edge (e) and surface (s) interactions. The subfigures depict different configurations: Nb2C with Flu at the edge (Nb2C-e-Flu) and surface (Nb2C-s-Flu), Nb2CO2 with Flu at the edge (Nb2CO2-e-Flu) and surface (Nb2CO2-s-Flu), and Nb2CF2 with Flu at the edge (Nb2CF2-e-Flu) and surface (Nb2CF2-s-Flu). These configurations highlight the potential influence of surface terminations (O and F) and adsorption sites on the drug-loading capacity. Table 2 quantifies the adsorption energies (Eads) and the closest distances (dQD-Flu) between the Flu drug and the Nb2C-based materials, providing insights into the interaction strength and stability for each configuration. The Eads are calculated using the formula: (Eads = EQD-Flu – EQDs- EFlu) where EQD-Flu is the electronic energy of the QD after loading the Flu drug, EQDs is the electronic energy of the QD before adsorption of Flu, and EFlu is the electronic energy of the Flu drug.

Nb2C-e-Flu exhibits moderate adsorption energy of −3.576 eV compared to Nb2C-s-Flu and a close interaction distance of 2.132 Å, indicating a stable edge site interaction. Nb2C-s-Flu exhibits the highest adsorption energy at −6.775 eV, which is significantly higher than the typical adsorption energy threshold of −2.00 eV [16], with a distance of 2.053 Å, indicating an exceptionally strong interaction and high stability of Flu on the Nb2C surface. Nb2CO2-e-Flu and Nb2CO2-s-Flu demonstrate reduced adsorption energies of −2.163 eV and −0.933 eV, with corresponding distances of 2.157 Å and 2.800 Å. These results indicate less favorable interactions due to oxygen termination, especially at the surface site. Nb2CF2-e-Flu and Nb2CF2-s-Flu also show weakened interactions with adsorption energies of −2.067 eV and −0.621 eV, and distances of 2.131 Å and 2.725 Å, respectively, suggesting that fluorine termination further reduces interaction strength, particularly at the surface site. The findings from Fig. 4 and Table 2 suggest that Nb2C-e-Flu and Nb2C-s-Flu exhibit strong interactions and high stability, making Nb2C unsuitable for efficient drug delivery, as it may inhibit the release of Flu at target sites. In contrast, Nb2CO2 and Nb2CF2, especially at the surface site with lower adsorption energies, present more favorable characteristics for drug delivery applications [16]. The reduced adsorption strength observed with O and F terminations highlights the importance of optimizing surface chemistry to achieve the desired drug-loading capacity. These insights are crucial for developing Nb2C-based drug delivery systems, ensuring effective and targeted delivery of Flu for cancer treatment. The presence of functional groups, such as O and F, plays a critical role in altering the surface chemistry of materials like Nb2C. These groups modify the electronic structure and charge distribution of the surface, which in turn influences the adsorption strength of molecules like Flu. Oxygen termination, for example, can reduce the interaction strength by creating less favorable binding sites, while fluorine termination further weakens interactions, especially at surface sites. These changes in surface properties are essential for fine-tuning drug delivery systems, as they determine the loading capacity and release behavior of the drug at targeted sites, ensuring the effective delivery of Flu for cancer therapy.

To connect the adsorption energies with practical implications for drug release mechanisms and stability under physiological conditions, it is important to consider how the strength of drug-MXene interactions influences both controlled release and the material's performance in biological environments. High Eads suggests strong drug binding, which can slow down drug release, providing sustained therapeutic effects. However, if the Eads is too strong, it could prevent the drug from being released at the target site. On the other hand, moderate Eads allow for more controlled release, making them ideal for drug delivery applications. Furthermore, the stability of Nb2C-based materials, influenced by adsorption energies, ensures that the materials remain intact during circulation, preventing premature degradation. The strong stability, coupled with efficient drug adsorption, makes these materials suitable candidates for drug delivery under physiological conditions. The materials' structural stability also prevents unwanted side reactions, improving the therapeutic efficacy and safety of the system both the binding energies and the physiological stability of the materials.

Fig. 5 (a–f) illustrates the electronic characteristics, including the distribution of HOMO/LUMO, HOMO energy, LUMO energy, and the Eg, for Nb2C and its derivatives following the loading of the Flu drug at both edge (e) and surface (s) positions. As presented in Fig. 5, the HOMO and LUMO distributions are mainly localized on the drug carrier, with the Flu drug itself not contributing significantly to these orbitals. When comparing Figs. 3 and 5, it is evident that the adsorption of the Flu drug at the edge and surface sites of Nb2C-based materials induces notable changes in the HOMO and LUMO distributions. Moreover, the Eg values are affected by drug adsorption at both positions, except for Nb2CO2 and Nb2CF2 at surface sites. In these cases, the Eg values remain unchanged at 1.496 eV and 1.314 eV, respectively. The stability observed implies a weak interaction between the Flu drug and the Nb2CO2-s and Nb2CF2-s surfaces, highlighting their potential as effective candidates for drug delivery applications, thanks to their stable electronic properties after adsorption. On the other hand, a notable decrease in the Eg value is observed in Nb2C-e after Flu adsorption, where the Eg decreases from 0.938 eV to 0.349 eV. This indicates strong adsorption of the Flu drug, making Nb2C-e less suitable for drug delivery applications. This analysis underscores the important impact of Flu drug adsorption on the electronic structure of Nb2C-based materials, highlighting specific configurations that maintain stability and suggesting potential for practical applications in targeted drug delivery.

Fig. 6 illustrates the calculated absorption spectra of pristine Nb2C and its derivatives (Nb2CO2 and Nb2CF2), both before and after the adsorption of Flu at the edge and surface sites. The absorption spectrum reveals significant changes in the electronic structure and optical properties due to passivation and Flu adsorption. Pristine Nb2C shows a unique absorption peak, highlighting its natural electronic transition characteristics. When modified with O and F atoms to form Nb2CO2 and Nb2CF2, the absorption profiles change significantly compared to the original Nb2C. This indicates that the introduction of these passivated atoms causes substantial changes in the electronic structure of Nb2C. Additionally, when Flu is adsorbed at the edge and surface sites of these materials, the absorption spectra shift noticeably. These shifts suggest that the interaction between Flu molecules and Nb2C-based materials affects their electronic transitions.

Table 3 displays the calculated values for excited state (ES), maximum wavelength (λ max), transition energy (TE), electronic transition (ET), oscillator strength (f), and transition coefficient (TC) for Nb2C and its derivatives, both before and after the adsorption of Flu at the edge and surface sites. Pristine Nb2C has an exciting state at 33, with a λ max of 2612.00 nm and a TE of 0.475 eV, characterized by an ET H-3→L+6 with a very low f (0.001) and a TC of 0.382. Nb2CO2 shows a significant blue shift with a λ max of 764.76 nm and a higher TE of 1.621 eV, with a transition H-3→L+4 and an oscillator strength of 0.014 and TC of 0.397, indicating more probable transitions compared to Nb2C. Nb2CF2 exhibits a λ max of 1108.71 nm and a TE of 1.118 eV, characterized by an electronic transition H-5→L+2 with a low oscillator strength (0.001) and a TC of 0.491.

The adsorption of Flu on Nb2C and its derivatives results in slight changes in the maximum wavelengths and transition energies. For Nb2C-e-Flu and Nb2C-s-Flu, the maximum wavelengths are 2635.37 nm and 2505.61 nm, respectively, with transition energies of 0.492 eV and 0.495 eV, both having very low oscillator strengths and transition coefficients. Flu adsorption on Nb2CO2 (Nb2CO2-e-Flu and Nb2CO2-s-Flu) causes minor changes in optical properties, with maximum wavelengths close to pristine Nb2CO2 and slight differences in transition energies and oscillator strengths. Similarly, Flu adsorption on Nb2CF2 (Nb2CF2-e-Flu and Nb2CF2-s-Flu) results in minor changes in maximum wavelengths and transition energies, with slight modifications in electronic transitions and oscillator strengths. The minor shifts in optical and electronic properties of Nb2C-based materials upon Flu adsorption suggest that these materials retain their electronic behavior, making them suitable for drug delivery applications. The low oscillator strengths and transition coefficients imply minimal photonic interactions, reducing the risk of phototoxicity in biological environments. These small property changes also suggest the potential for controlled drug release, which is critical for precise targeting and reducing systemic toxicity in treatments like cancer therapy. Additionally, these shifts may be useful for real-time monitoring of drug delivery, enhancing both therapeutic and diagnostic capabilities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Flu drug interactions using NCI plots and surfaces
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 and S1 (a-c) present Non-Covalent Interaction (NCI) plots and surfaces depicting the interactions between a Flu drug and three materials: Nb2C, Nb2CO2, and Nb2CF2, at both edge and surface sites. The color scheme in these plots reveals the nature of the interactions between the drug and the materials. For surface interactions (Fig. 7 ), the Nb2C-s-Flu plot Fig. 7c shows a distinct blue color between the Flu drug and the Nb2C, indicating strong attractive interactions likely due to hydrogen bonding. This suggests favorable incorporation of the Flu drug at the surface of Nb2C. In contrast, the Nb2CO2-s-Flu and Nb2CF2-s-Flu plots (Fig. 7 (a and b))exhibit a predominantly green color between the Flu drug and the respective materials, signifying weaker interactions such as van der Waals (vdW) forces. While minimal blue regions hint at weak hydrogen bonding, they are significantly less prominent compared to Nb2C-s-Flu, suggesting a weaker affinity of the Flu drug for the surfaces of Nb2CO2 and Nb2CF2.

For edge interactions (Figure S1 (a-c)), t he Nb2C-e-Flu plot displays a green color between the Flu drug and the Nb atoms, indicating weaker interactions at the edge compared to the surface. Notably, the Nb2CO2-e-Flu and Nb2CF2-e-Flu plots lack the green color observed in Nb2C-e-Flu, suggesting an even weaker interaction between the drug and the edges of Nb2CO2 and Nb2CF2. However, the presence of blue color in all edge plots (Nb2CF2-e-Flu, Nb2CO2-e-Flu, and Nb2C-e-Flu) signifies strong attractive interactions, possibly due to hydrogen bonding with specific functional groups on the Flu drug interacting with the edges of these materials.

Overall, the observations suggest a weaker interaction between the Flu drug and the surface of Nb2CF2 and Nb2CO2. Additionally, Nb2C appears to have a stronger affinity for the Flu drug at both edge and surface sites compared to Nb2CO2 and Nb2CF2. By examining the NCI plots, we gain valuable insights into how the Flu drug interacts with these materials. This information is crucial in understanding drug delivery and material design processes, where interfacial interactions play a significant role. Based on the interaction strengths observed, Nb2CF2-s and Nb2CO2-s are recommended as the best candidates for drug delivery. These materials exhibit weaker interactions with the Flu drug, which can be advantageous for controlled drug release and minimizing potential adverse effects associated with strong binding.

Table 4 presents a comprehensive analysis of the natural charge, natural population, and natural electronic configuration of the O7 atom in the Flu drug before and after its interaction with Nb2C and its derivatives. The natural charge on the O7 atom shows slight variations upon interaction with these materials. Notably, the negative value of the natural charge on O7 in the Flu drug increases after loading in all studied compounds except in Nb2C-e, where the negative value decreases. This suggests that, in most cases, the Flu drug acts as an electron acceptor while the Nb2C-based carriers act as electron donors. However, in the Nb2C-e-Flu complex, the Flu drug acts as an electron donor, and Nb2C-e acts as an acceptor. The natural population analysis provides a detailed breakdown into core, valence, and Rydberg contributions, offering insights into electron distribution. The core electrons remain constant at 2.000 across all complexes, indicating no significant changes in the inner shell electrons. Valence electrons show slight variations, ranging from 6.613 to 6.654, indicating minor reorganization of the outer shell electrons due to interaction with Nb2C derivatives. For instance, the valence electron population in Nb2C-s-Flu is 6.654, reflecting an increased electron density in the valence shell, which may be attributed to a stronger interaction with the Nb2C surface.

The natural electronic configuration provides a detailed orbital composition for the O7 atom. In the Flu drug, the configuration is [core]2S(1.74)2p(4.89)3p(0.01). After interaction, it changes slightly: in Nb2C-e-Flu, it is [core]2S(1.74)2p(4.88)3p(0.01), and in Nb2C-s-Flu, it is [core]2S(1.67)2p(4.99)3p(0.01). These minor differences in the 2S and 2p orbital populations suggest changes in the hybridization state and electron density distribution in the O7 atom. For instance, the Nb2C-s-Flu complex shows a higher 2p population (4.99), indicating increased electron density in the p orbitals, likely due to a stronger interaction with the Nb2C surface. In summary, the loading of the Flu drug onto Nb2C and its derivatives results in subtle but significant changes in the natural charge, population, and electronic configuration of the O7 atom. These changes, particularly in the valence electron population and orbital configurations, provide insights into the electronic behavior and potential reactivity of the Flu drug when interfaced with Nb2C derivatives. Understanding these effects is crucial for optimizing the design and functionality of drug delivery systems and nanomaterials in biomedical applications.

This study provides comprehensive insights into the structural, electronic, optical, and adsorption properties of Nb2C MXene and its functionalized derivatives Nb2CO2 and Nb2CF2. The findings reveal that functionalization with oxygen and fluorine atoms enhances stability and modifies the vibrational spectra, confirming successful incorporation and thermodynamic stability. The electronic property analysis reveals marked variations in HOMO-LUMO distributions and energy gaps, with Nb2CO2 exhibiting the most stable electronic configuration. Drug-loading studies with fluorouracil (Flu) highlight strong interactions and high adsorption energy for Nb2C, making it less suitable for drug-delivery applications. In contrast, Nb2CO2 and Nb2CF2 demonstrate advantageous properties for drug delivery, particularly at surface sites, making them more suitable for efficient and controlled therapeutic applications. Optical properties further support the potential of Nb2CO2 and Nb2CF2 for targeted drug delivery, demonstrating notable modifications in the absorption spectrum and electronic transitions. Non-Covalent Interaction (NCI) studies provide additional insights into electronic charge distribution changes upon drug adsorption, reinforcing the understanding of interaction mechanisms. These findings underscore the importance of functionalization and surface chemistry optimization for developing effective MXene-based drug delivery systems. The potential of Nb2C and its derivatives is further emphasized in enhancing drug delivery efficiency through improved stability, controlled release, and optimized electronic properties. This makes them promising candidates for therapeutic applications, particularly in the realm of targeted drug delivery and precision medicine.
